narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles of past students. The model estimated a 96.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which directly led to the rejection of your application, as schools prioritize admitting students likely to succeed and avoid high-risk profiles. The three most influential factors in this decision were your academic decile rankings from earlier school years, your LSAT score, and your Year 3 decile specifically, all of which pushed the prediction strongly toward failure rather than success. Most critically, your Year 3 decile ranking of 1—far below the average of about 5.5—signaled to the model a significant dip in performance compared to peers, making it the top driver toward predicting bar failure. Your LSAT score of 26, well under the dataset average of 36.7, was the second biggest concern, as lower scores like yours consistently correlate with bar struggles in the model's analysis of similar students. Even your Year 1 decile of 1, again much lower than the typical 5.7, added further weight toward the failure prediction, despite your stronger undergrad record. Your middle family income bracket and full-time student status—common among 92.7% of applicants—didn't sway things much, as they align with the norm and weren't key influencers here. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often show higher deciles and LSATs that pull predictions toward passing, your profile stood out as riskier across these core academic signals. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background, but a data-driven flag that these early and standardized metrics don't match patterns of bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, while reflecting on what might have impacted those low school deciles—perhaps targeted tutoring or stronger study habits could help rebuild that foundation. Strengthening these areas could shift future predictions in your favor, opening doors at other programs or upon reapplication. With your above-average undergrad GPA as a solid base, you're already ahead in one key area—keep building on that resilience.
